replies.author: @ssharp755
replies.text:
@DJWHITE_ You realise that literally nothing you said there has any bearing whatsoever on the point I was making?  

Plus the idea that 'nobody ever watches a movie more than once these days'  is completely invalidated by the occasional billion dollar stand out or the Barbenheimer phenomenon etc - the key is audience interest and quality would be the more reliable means of engendering that than lucking out on your marketing strategy. 

All the shenanigans around attempting to manufacture consent via review  manipulation, social media engineering and etc are because they, at least unconsciously, realise they are no longer able to produce quality, and THAT is because of the ossified nature of the industry itself, with small minded people, grasping at small time goals, covering their own back etc. They don't care about us, agreed, but they also don't really care about making money, so long as they're covering their own asses, shifting the blame to the audience etc and pretending that it's just impossible to recognise the formula's that would actually bring that cash rolling in because it goes against their idealogical worldview.

* Edit to add *  You're not wrong to suggest that the market has changed and perhaps the overall profit share would fall naturally so far as box office is concerned, but it's not the pursuit of that profit that is the primary fault, because their internal practices are demonstrably and utterly sabotaging their ability to maximise whatever potential actually exists at the baseline. When the agenda is more camoflagued or gets out of the way, audiences flock back to theatres, even for decidedly mediocre fare (Superman 2025, Deadpool & Wolverine), imagine how much MORE money those might have made if they had been able to capture a more iconic status with the populace.

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text:
To be fair, you have a lot to do with what your kid likes. You shaped him and influence him, hopefully. Not saying you are wrong but I see people using that as a demonstration all the time, but it doesn't necessarily prove anything except that children will usually pick up on what their parents like and don't like and emulate them. Which is why setting a good example is a primary part of being a good parent. Unless you carefully create the experiment by never favoring any particular type of film, even carefully guarding your facial expressions and other cues, and then you sit down and watch old and new films together, and then ask which ones they liked better, and they still say they like the old ones better, it doesn't really prove anything. 
Not that I am saying you _should_ do that, you are doing exactly what you should be doing, influencing them in the right way. Because it is easy for unformed minds to fall for the crap Hollywood makes today. It's got lots of excitement and mindless humor and stuff that kids don't necessarily see anything wrong with unless they are taught why other things can be better. In fact I think a large part of what's wrong with modern movies is that they are well aware of the tendency people have to use TV as a babysitter and tutor for their kids so they don't have to do all that work, and they make them intentionally appealing to kids even when they aren't supposed to be kids movies, because they know kids 8 of kids will he watch them anyway. But they just ruin what the film is supposed to be with a lot of silly stuff that wasn't necessary.

​​@eyedunno8462 yes, and the adaptation was presented so well that I cry at the end of Return of the King /every time/ even if I hadn't just marathoned the whole trilogy lol

Just so we're all clear, I hate when adaptations cut parts out of books and change things, but given the massive work that the Lord of the Rings books are, I still think the movies were probably the best adaptation we'll ever get. There are scenes cut from Fellowship, for example, that I think are perfectly understandable not to keep in a movie that was already going to be /very/ long. Even really good movies can't be too long; people's bladders aren't that big 😂

author: @flova1084
text: In trying to sell a script, I came to realize just how saturated the creative landscape has become. Hollywood no longer needs to look beyond its inner circle — it's easier to rely on a friend of a friend or someone's cousin than to sift through the billions of scripts floating around. The result is a kind of creative inbreeding, where the same ideas are recycled by people too used to their own echo chamber, blind to fresh voices and the world outside

replies.author: @XanthisBoston
replies.text:
There was always saturation.  1950's Hollywood made about 350 films/year, most of which were sh*t, about half of which no longer exist in any form.  In the 1990's, there were 450 theatrical releases per year, plus 500 - 800 direct-to-video.  Each year, in this ocean of junk, about *5 - 10* films were actually _good,_ and maybe *1 - 2* classics.  We're still churning out the junk, but where are the gems??

Personally, I think the artistic vision is being filtered out: every line of dialogue is run through 10 committees until it turns into oatmeal.

replies.author: @willie_the_monkey_king
replies.text:
You also forgot “I’m always angry,” “puny god,” and the exchange “I’ve got an army.” “We’ve got a Hulk.” Those were at the top of my head and that was in 2012. I can also quote the memorable lines from Mando season 1 to further my point. 
It’s a very surface commentary. While when it comes to mainstream movies having that malaise, there are a glut of original indie movies out there. The point Drinker missed in his video is that around the 2000’s we have eroded the monoculture we had. There was/is less and less common cultural touchstones. This can all be traced as the flipside of having less and less gatekeepers. When you have too many options for entertainment you divide the audience to splinters.

A lot of these are only memorable to those of us who liked or at least saw the movies.  People who NEVER saw a Rocky movie know what "Yo Adrian!" is. 
   "Make my day" and "I'll be back" were EVERYWHERE in the mid-1980's -- a country singer named TG Sheppard recorded a SONG of "Make my day".   (Which, now that I think of it, I still remember verbatim.)
   Even the best lines from modern movies are still niche memes for the fan community, not significant to the broader culture -- if there IS a broader culture anymore.

replies.author: @EvenTheDogAgrees
replies.text:
@Bhavyo Thank you for pointing that out. Every time people complain about "today's music" or "todays movies" or whatever, I'm usually the one who has to correct them: "today's _mainstream_ music/movies".

And guess what? Sturgeon's law isn't exactly new. 90% of everything is crap now, but was crap in 2015, 2005, 1995, ... too! Now, while the 80's and especially 90's were a golden age for quality cinema/music surfacing closer to the mainstream, producing a ton of classics in both cinema and music, there was also a lot of nonsense. We all remember The Matrix. But who remembers Airbud?

replies.author: @zip10031
replies.text:
It's interesting that things which are seen in hindsight as quintessentially 1990s weren't really a thing at all for a lot of people at the time. Maybe it's just a few American cities thinking that they represent the entire world? I lived through the 1990s (in Australia) and had never even heard about JNCO jeans, and I don't remember anyone wearing them, but they now apparently helped define the era. They certainly don't appear in any of the photo albums we have.

author: @KingKel500-u4r
text:
From the humble perspective of a 21 year old man, I think the issue lies in the philosophy behind modern film. 
It’s not about making art, it’s about making money or pushing a message. The corporations don’t want memorable movies, they want profitable ones. Movies that are both memorable and profitable are rarer than a blue moon, especially in the 2020’s.

I wasn’t even alive when Jaws or Indiana Jones or Back to the Future released, but I miss them. I miss movies that were made with passion instead of an overused formula.

replies.author: @pamelab7235
replies.text:
Well, I think movie studios always wanted to make money.  I can’t quite see a Louis B. Mayer or Jack Warner being indifferent to the bottom line (that was what art house movies were for😜). I think the difference is that more people had standards and sought excellence.  Movie making was both an art and a craft, an industry filled with skilled and talented people for whom “good enough” was not good enough.  I think over reliance on technology makes people lazy and sloppy, and it shows.

Every frame of the original Disney film Snow White was hand drawn.  THAT is quality.

replies.author: @aldunlop4622
replies.text: I'm old enough to be your dad, I think the problem is the older generation tried to wrap their kids in cotton wool and bubble wrap so nothing could hurt them, and that led to them simply taking no risks. Reward only comes with risk. We must be allowed to fall and fail, so we can learn. Gen X wasn't parented much at all. Our parents grew up during WW2 they just expected us to get up, carry on and not annoy them. This led to Boomers and GenX "overloving" their kids, giving them all the affection we never got. But it's just led to the riskless state we see today. I was just watching a video called 80 hits of 1980 and what struck me most about the music was the variety. In a single year there was disco, punk, soft and hard rock, metal, synth rock, new romantics etc etc. Everyone was experimenting. You just don't see it today.

The Indiana Jones movies weren't exactly high art either, and were another franchise of summer popcorn blockbusters. The key is getting someone with passion for the project behind the camera, and actors whowant to be there, not just for the pay check. When script writers and directors don't care, that really shows in the end product. There's no passion anymore, only cynical apathy.

Music is only dead if you only listen to the default Pop radio crap, there are so many great modern artists with a whole variety of sounds. For instance Maidavale, Svartanatt, and Black Star Riders are all producing fresh heavier sounds that really belong to many decades prior.  Along with the old geriatric punks, metalheads and rockers that are often still going, or at least only recently deceased, and have been producing new stuff for all that time since they were 'mainstream' enough for most folks to have heard of them. Then you get the crazy fools like Windrose and Alestorm going Dwarven and Pirate themed for fun! You'd never know if you only listen the Cowel'n'co manufactured soulless stuff, but there is a whole world of good stuff out there and the internet has largely allowed it spread.
And the shorter form video stuff is flourishing without being all corporate slop, really its only cinema where to do it properly you do tend to need budgets with an extra zero or two compared to the cost of the whole run for a TV series that is dying a fair bit, and that had been dying even back when Lord of The Rings happened - the risk adverse lower effort sequel crap had definitely started by then.

author: @HardAsStoneRN
text: I think another problem is that it seems that too often people from the entertainment industry, from movies, TV, and everything else, just don't seem to have the same passion, love, or respect for what they work on anymore or that they did decades ago. It's become pretty obvious in the last decade from all the creators and workers who actively hate/disrespect their audience and the source material they've been given, to the lack of excitement that many actors seem to be experiencing in many of their works and also the almost routine-like releases of tv, movies, games, songs, all of it becoming more like a chore instead of a big event. Basically, the entertainment industry has fallen into this weird apathetic routine of getting the next job or release done and just move on to the next. And as a result they don't really care about what they do and audiences don't seem to care that much either. This is how the entertainment industry dies, not with a bang, but the softest whimper.

replies.author: @blueiris2025

I think the problem is the culture itself. They've become so bogged down with trying to meet DEI demands that the real storytellers, script writers, and novelists are being suppressed.

replies.author: @waylander9265
replies.text: I can’t say if this applies to every case but I think a key change is that it used to be that creatives pitched adaptations that they thought would make great movies e.g Peter Jackson with LoTR. This wasn’t always the case but the big successes tended to be this way. Nowadays it’s nearly always companies hiring writers to adapt their IPs, look at the Rings of Power. The first approach had passionate people driving the project, the second is someone doing a job to pay bills

replies.author: @Enigma75614
replies.text: Agreed

replies.author: @BubblegumCrisis2
replies.text:
@waylander9265 Although visual entertainment - movies specfically  - is by nature highly collaborative, the corporate, committee model (having like 10 - albeit very talented and passionate - writers) to script writing doesn't and cannot work. The approach seeks to negotiate, thereby normalising the narrative. Be 'palatable' to as many people as possible. Earn money even if nobody cares (yes my mom dragged me to see that Lively/Baldoni film in the cinema for 'ladies night' and it was truly awful. But hey it made dosh).

Great tales on the other hand, are mostly written by individuals (like LOTR) and individuals are spiky, unruly and often not 'normal' (all those morally reprehensible authors, true). Their stories move - not all of us - but some of us. Citizen Kane anyone?

replies.author: @thesouthernwake
replies.text: I would add to this and say it's afflicted by the same thing that is hurting so much of our western cultural: we have no shame anymore. These people that should consider themselves artists, aren't ashamed of making crap. Remember when artists were stereotyped as idealistic and hard to work with bc they wanted perfection. Now they want 18 writers in a room with benefits and PTO. In the end, I blame the accumulation of power for political purposes - across not just govts but corporations. Look at our entertainment in the US - when ppl stopped watching nightly news anchors, the corps just moved the cultural narratives to movies, sports, and gaming. They gatekeep real artists and individual creators. You aren't allowed to be successful w/o their permission, bc then you have a voice they might not approve of. Look at the Oscars - it's just a big club of chosen ppl of varying talent.

replies.author: @Contraltissimo
replies.text:
I'm happy to report at least just one exception!
I finally watched Transformers One (2024) the other week, and wow, as a long-time Transformers fan, I could _absolutely_ feel the love and respect everyone involved had for the original lore, and how they knew exactly how to speak to fans, and how _not_ to speak to them. I'm just sad it had such abysmal marketing - otherwise I'm certain I would have seen it at the cinema at least five times!
No more gratuitous Baysplosions, just a really well-crafted character story around some core Cybertronian history. Even if you're not a Transformers fan, still a great movie, and worth a watch!

It seems like the real problem is that the “block busters” have lost passion.

replies.author: @niymeh342
replies.text: @blueiris2025 It's not even DEI, it's purely profit.  They don't hire people with talent, because talent requires effort, risk, cost, and a more limited audience. replies.author: @niymeh342
replies.text: @Contraltissimo And yet their own marketing department didn't bother marketing it much, and when it did, it did so in the most hamfisted way possible that completely misrepresented the film. replies.author: @niymeh342
replies.text:
@Kaiserboo1871 Exactly.  Zack Snyder is at best a one-trick pony, yet he keeps getting to make films.  Michael Bay can do great explosions but nothing else except fellating the American military, but he keeps getting to make films.  Rian Johnson wants to 'subvert expectations' regardless of whether it makes sense in the plot or is a good story, and he keeps getting to make films.  JJ Abrahms can SET UP interesting mysteries and fun plot points, but has no idea how to actually reach any kind of conclusion in any way, shape, or form... yet he keeps getting to make films.

And then even the people who DO have talent, once they get big enough, end up surrounded by yes-men so they never get any kind of constructive criticism, just constant affirmations that whatever they decide is the best because they're deciding it.  And that means they end up going so far up themselves but as long as they make the studios money, that's fine.  But the moment the money slows down, they're out.

replies.author: @dr.juerdotitsgo5119
replies.text: @XanthisBoston What you CANNOT do (Hays Code) and what you HAVE to do (DEI) are very different things. A great artist finds a way around the first by using creativity, subtlety and wit - the most notorious method being the "Lubitsch Touch". The second one however cripples your story before its conception.

replies.author: @AnimationVault

replies.author: @XanthisBoston
replies.text: @paultullysafari The OP is onto something: none of these characters could be written the same way today.  But you're right that it's more than _just_ the fear of cancellation.  Movies have gotten steadily more expensive, and studios are afraid of failure for _any_ reason.  As a result, dialogue is written and re-written by committee.  Each line is chewed over until it turns into flavorless oatmeal.

replies.author: @XanthisBoston
replies.text: ​@dr.juerdotitsgo5119 That's a _very_ good point.  You've changed my mind: I agree.  To be fair, the Hays code had a few "must-do's" (e.g., villains were not allowed to have a happy ending, traditional values had to be upheld, etc.), but that didn't poison character and story development like DEI does.  The Hays code could the satisfied by striking the villain down with a "bolt from the blue" at the end of the movie.  But DEI poisons the script from the beginning.

replies.author: @kargen1963
replies.text: Yup, this is the problem right here

replies.author: @XanthisBoston
replies.text: @justforever96 If we read the OP a little more charitably, when he says they're afraid of offending "someone," he means offending the _wrong people._  In other words, there's an ideological agenda (and overall studio fear of failure) that drives and constrains how characters are written.  Few of the characters mentioned could be written the same way today.  Therefore, when a character in a modern movie says, _"Make my day,"_ it's not memorable because _the character_ wasn't memorable, having been written by committee.  Modern characters are largely oatmeal.

@jjc5407
You and people like you are the exact reason why movies these days are so watered down and inoffensive. Ask the parents first? Jesus. I saw A Nightmare on Elm Street 4 when I was 11 years old while at a sleepover at a friend’s house. We didn’t ask his parents first, we just put it in the VCR and let it rip. We all sat back and immersed our young selves in the story playing out on the TV, and when it was over we simply turned it off and went to sleep. None of us were scarred for life, we didn’t immediately go out looking to recreate what we saw on screen and we didn’t hole up inside the house for days on end ruminating on the need for more parental supervision in our lives. These days everyone is so scared to offend someone else and the movies we get are exactly the same. Stop clutching your pearls and let kids be kids. I guarantee that they’ll be better off in the long run.

replies.author: @SeasideDetective2
replies.text:
It's worth pointing out that all of the films Drinker quoted in the opening were from no earlier than the 1970s. Movies with spoken dialogue had been around for several decades prior to then. Granted, 1930s thru '60s films also have many memorable lines of dialogue...but then, what about silent films? Almost none of the lines (or intertitles) from those early movies are remembered by most people today, even by many silent movie buffs. But were not many of those also great films?

There was never a rule that said movies had to be "quippy." Clever dialogue became a sort of cinematic tradition as a backlash to the ponderousness and stilted language of silent cinema, but for a while that just resulted in movies becoming celluloid plays, with characters engaging in repartee for long stretches of action. That's fine for Oscar Wilde, but not for 20th or 21st-century entertainment. And who said movies had to have lots of dialogue at all? Much of the appeal of action movies, for example, is that they show far more than they tell. Most screenwriters probably never write a quotable line throughout their entire careers, but that doesn't mean their screenplays can't be good.

replies.author: @RussoEntertainment
replies.text: I get that, I do. But as a filmmaker, I wonder what might change your mind about that? I'm genuinely curious because I think a lot of movie-goers have hit that same wall -- they've stopped even paying attention to what's on the market. And I can't blame them. But if I were to say, I've got a comedy coming out on August 5th, would you be open to looking at the trailer? My goal is to make films people actually want to see. I'm trying to figure out how to spark excitement about my films, and I want to hear from the audience what their take is. What would get you excited about a movie again?

replies.author: @krslavin
replies.text: @RussoEntertainment I often hate the trailers - bang-bang "music", dizzying scene changes, no idea who the central characters are, or what they represent. Are they even likable?

replies.author: @dr.juerdotitsgo5119
replies.text: @RussoEntertainment Speaking for myself, not making your movie look like a videogame is a great start. Then stop the frenetic editing, stop the constant use of pop music and invasive soundtrack to artificially evoke emotions, stop the gritty lighting unless you're making a 1940's black & white film noir, let the shots take their time and allow us to savour the cinematography, return to blocking instead of just shot/reverse shot in extreme close-up, make the characters more stoic and remember that not saying something can be much more powerful than spelling it out- rely on subtext... I could go on 🤣

replies.author: @RussoEntertainment
replies.text: @krslavin I agree. Trailers these days lack focus a lot of the time. I tried to do better with mine, hopefully I succeeded: https://youtu.be/DM_GyEVv7p4

replies.author: @RussoEntertainment
replies.text: @dr.juerdotitsgo5119 Very good feedback. The way trailers are pieced together these days is definitely a problem in many cases. And I so agree about the lighting. That's one thing I love about older movies, especially a lot of them from the 80s -- the lighting lent itself to a warmer, more inviting look and feel. I don't know if that's the best way to describe it, but anyway, I hear you on that one. Subtext, also a huge must for a good story. Modern writers are bad about exposition dumps. They haven't learned how to communicate through subtelty and nuance. For what it's worth, I tried to do better with the trailer for my film. Maybe it worked, maybe it didn't: https://youtu.be/DM_GyEVv7p4.

"The unknown future rolls toward us."
"I face it, for the first time, with a sense of hope."

author: @sergeantsnugglez3228
text: Modern times is a fucking shame.  I'm 41 and have 3 kids ages 9,7,4 and I feel so bad for them.  I mean they don't know any better, but I know how much fun/love/excitement they would have had if they were born in my era 80's/90's.  I have shown them movies from my time, and they play all my retro games, but it's just not the same.  No Saturday morning cartoons, no good malls to go to, no more arcades, no weekly good movie releases, no good kid's/family sitcoms, no neighborhood kids playing outside (except mine), hell man I could write pages more if I actually thought about it.  I don't let it be known to them their time sucks, but man it's hard to keep it exciting.  Everything today is disposable and forgettable.

replies.author: @UnlikelyExplanation
replies.text:
On the more optimistic side AI is going to  give YouTubers like The Drinker  the power to make series and feature length content, there will be a few diamonds like him who shine through in a never ending sea of content, but the audiences are never likely to approach the levels of the past as the vast amount of content is just too fragmented to provide a unifying cultural touch point like the UK and I assume the USA had back in the day, in the UK at least that has already narrowed down to only sports like Premier League and Formula 1 i.e. the last thing the computers cant do

But Hollywood will be reduced to licencing their content so AI can train on it to make personalised remakes of old franchises, designed for your tastes. Want a series 8 of Star Trek the Next Generation? Or a crossover with Battlestar Galactica? No problem. Some of the personalised content will be co-authored by humans or the AI will emulate their styles, for example say Ron Moore might write a short treatment for Star Trek Next Generation and you will be able to watch a Series 8 in 'his' style co produced by AI.

It's going to be crazy

replies.author: @dreadnoughtsea
replies.text: ​@ftftyffghfvghfcht6701First off, no Avatar 2 was not really memorable. Like at all, I can't even remember what the big story beats were I just remember it looked pretty while it happened. Second, it's not about the cool one liners- it's about what makes those lines memorable. Modern movies have those lines too, but they're hollow. In older movies the story behind those lines gave them emotional weight and made them meaningful, therefore memorable. Now they just want to get the big flashy epic sounding one liner, but they don't pour their heart and soul into the rest of the movie so when the characters say it there is nothing to back it up and it's meaningless and forgettable

my brother likes 2049 better, but I disagreed.


Rutger Hauer is underrated.

HAVE YOU SEEN HIM HOBO WITH A SHOTGUN(2011)?

low-budget- raimi-esque action/comed, with Hauer as the Hobo in question.

It's wild.


right before the big battle, Rutger hauer has this line :

as he speaks through the glass to a room full of defenseless babies in the NICU, sleeping
in their hospital cradles.


" a long time ago, I was one of you.
All brand new and perfect
no mistakes no regrets
people look at you and think 
of how wonderful your future will be
they want you to be something special 

like a doctor or a lawyer
I hate to tell you this
but if you grow up here
you're more likely to
wind up selling your bodies on the streets
or shooting dope from dirty needles in a bus stop.

and if you're successful, you'll make money
selling junk to crack heads.
and you won't think twice about killing someones wife
because you wont even know what was wrong in the first place.

or maybe,  You'll end up like me. 

A HOBO WITH A SHOTGUN.

I hope you can do better.

You are the future. "

MUSIC RISES.

shootout sequence rutger haur vs the plague. 

worth a watch.

replies.author: @MaxFreibier
replies.text: Many of my older friends (i was born i the late 80ths) who experienced the 70 to 90th pitty me, when i tell them from my concerts and festivals. They still cant believe, everything got so commercialized, unpersonal and closed up. Other way around, i cant hardly imagina their times. Sneaking over the fence, finding everyday a new girl for the night, buy a car to the festival for a crate of beer or simply drive what you found in the dumpster. Special the times in reunited Berlin around 1992 must have been wild. They broke up old factories left from the east system and celebrated big raves without any promoter, company or city permit. Unbelievable today

replies.author: @simondavis750
replies.text: 74 here. We went through so many of the great cultural zeitgeists and if you are feeling like me when seeing the state of entertainment today, am not impressed with this one. Remember that feeling on a Friday night going down to the video store and getting a well played copy of some weird sci-fi movie, and that bizarre feeling when watching teenagers today talking about this "lost classic". Nobody can get that feeling anymore because our media is streamed constantly, seeing that movie back then had a story around seeing it, it wasn't just something in between a makeup tutorial and someone lipsynching latest garbage song.

replies.author: @olliefoxx7165
replies.text: Thats a valid point. Yes, the way we consume media is different. Movies aren't as special bc there are so many choices. Covid destroyed going to the movies as a weekend event and things began being streamed. This took away an essential "group experience" our culture would share. However, an equally important part of the decline is the quality of the movies. Young people that only know today's media simply dont know how much better the movies used to be. Unless they watch older content they only have slop as their reference points. Our culture has decayed substantially. I see it across the board in all aspects. This is the great injustice to the youth. They have been handed the keys to a once glorious mansion thats full of rot, a collapsing basement, vermin infested, faulty wiring and a sagging roof. They dont know it's bad bc its all they've ever known and they deserved better.

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text: Yeah I kind of miss the days when we had a small shelf of VHS tapes at home and our local video store with a room full of movies. You just had to rewatch the good ones or go without, until you could remember every scene from your favorites. Same with music. You had to listen to your favorites over and over because it wasn't so easy or cheap to find new stuff. I like having all this music so easily available, but I also miss the intimate connection you made with your favorite albums when you had to listen to it over and over. I heard someone playing one of my old songs at work the other day and I immediately recognized it from about a second of vague audio, and started to sing the song, which I still remembered every word of even though I hadn't heard it in 15 years maybe.

author: @enochcarpenter1970
text:
It isn't just pop culture.   I've spent The last half decade working jobs where I travel around and do work for several customers a day,  mostly businesses. 

Everyone is cutting corners.  Abandoning wings of perfectly good buildings.  Working from home. Turning conference rooms into storage rooms. 

Everything is being streamlined,  and the sacrificial priority is interaction.   Culture is a byproduct of human interaction,  so it shouldn't be a surprise that virtualizing all interaction results in   hollow culture.

Everyone go read a story called "The Machine Stops" by EM Forster.
      In 1928, he predicted a form of social media, universal work-from-home, irrational fear-driven lockdowns from hypochondria, the decline of original content into endless rehashes of what had gone before, emotionless hook-ups facilitated by an algorithm, the decline of aesthetic standards into efficient homogeneity, climate alarmism, widespread loss of physical fitness, fragmentation of families into solitary households, and the worship of AI (of a sort).
    It actually ends on an optimistic note, of the "burn it all down and hope for better" sort.

author: @HowardLovecraft1936
text: In the 90s we all watched the same tv shows and movies. We talked about them at school and on weekends. My kids today have unlimited options with the internet, social media and streaming services.  The culture has splintered into infinite pieces and nothing is shared deeply. This is a result of the internet and multiculturalism, where shared values and interests have been demonised and a plural nihilism reigns. Glad I got to experience life as a teen in the 90s where depression and anxiety were rare.

replies.author: @flymeslowly
replies.text:
​​@MaxLittleBuddyAny addition, subtraction, comments, or opposition to what this person said just buttresses it further.

Invincible was able to stand out because what exactly happened was what this person just described - it was shared.

Online, offline, students, young adults, it was shared and talked about.

Infact now, its views don't peak like season 1 because it's not longer shared - people no longer talk about it like they did when it first came out.

Now we have many movies, series, shows, different genres, all dropping their shits on consumers with no quality control. You can only share so much with somebody interested in another show you are not.

So what do movie makers of this generation do, try to make a very very interesting movie that connects and is well thought of, this movie garners the interest of almost everyone and then it can be shared culturally (at least for a while)

Example

Openheimer last year 
Barbie last year
Invincibles that you mentioned
GoT
Superman 2025 (almost everyone's interested in the movie, haven't watched it, but I'm also interested to)

And so on....

In short, in a world were movies drop left and right in streaming services for consumers, as a movie maker, you can only strive to deliver a good product (infact, the best) and hope it catches the eyes of the majority.

author: @yeeeahbrah
text: It's everywhere though. Shared experiences have been killed by the internet and specifically social media. TV, films and music used to be much more concentrated in terms of who it reached and when. Everything is on demand and consumed individually. What was designed to bring us closer together has forced us further apart. Consequently the appetite for risk has declined now as it's so damn easy to lose people's attention in favour of something else.

replies.author: @justforever96
replies.text:
I can already see several problems with that theory. Although it's certainly possible, but probably within a larger, longer cycle that follows the same trend on a longer scale. It would also only be true in a very broad and general sense, depending on the exact time and place and situation, and only roughly fitting that time frame. 
Also does it apply to Asia and Africa, does Haiti have this same phenomenon? Is it demonstrable going back to 500BC in Syria, or does it only talk about the last couple hundred years in Western nations? replies.author: @thedemonthrone3463
replies.text:
@justforever96 The phenomenon can be summarized as "Bad times create strong men, strong men create good times, good times create weak men, weak men create bad times."
I think you can find that cycle repeats quite often throughout history and in different cultures, although I am only personally familiar with European history. I can certainly see that cycle appearing in ancient Greece and Rome.
Perhaps the modern age in the West has accelerated or accentuated the cycle. Our good times are certainly far more good than they would have been in the past, or in less technologically advanced cultures. And our bad times are capable of being far worse as well.
It does depend a bit on what you look at as major crises or turning points. The past 5-600 years of European history is filled with war and strife, yet we tend to look at only a handful of conflicts as major inflection points. Those points tend to line up with the cycle pretty well.
The American revolution and Civil wars? About 90 years apart.
The Napoleonic wars and WW1? About 100 years apart. The Spanish war of succession was about 100 years before the Napoleonic wars, although I'm not sure how impactful that was to Europe as a whole.

replies.author: @NicholasLeeson
replies.text: @justforever96 The book Generations went back to the early 1600s, and focused on America. However, its Turnings were loosely in-line with the whole of the West, due to the shared wars and shared economics. The book introduced what is referred to as the 4th Turning, which they predicted would begin around 2005, but instead that turned out to be 2008. The reason this happens over and over again, is due to the economics, and because of how you were raised. For example, if you were a child that grew up during the great depression and world war 2, your childhood would have been one of at least at lot of worry, possibly going hungry a lot, and possibly seeing a lot of people around you die. This would have had a massive impact on the development of their personality. Then the children born after the war, who saw none of this, would have grown up completely differently, and thus see the world differently. When they get to adulthood, you've suddenly got a very different adult hitting the world. They then have their own revolution, and essentially the next generation of children growing up kinda get ignored, which is Generation X. These kids had extreme freedom, which was kinda scarring on them as young child. Look up the Still Face Experiment if you want to see how ignoring a baby can effect them. And yet this freedom was very well received as teenagers. The scarring though then led to the currently helicopter parenting that we have now. Going back to the great depression and World War 2, prior to this we had a huge wealth gap (just as we have now), which is tracked by the Gini Coefficient. The wealth gap goes up and down over long periods all the time, with events like global wars and plagues wiping the slate clean and bringing the wealth gap back to a healthy level. However, over time wealth accumulates into fewer and fewer hands, until the wealth gap grows to such a extent that you start to see protests and countries attacking one another due to resource issues. Thus, the economics will always take us to a boiling point, until there's a reset, and these resets have an impact on the generations, similar to a sine or square wave. There are plenty of books talking about these cycles of history, so I'm inclined to believe it's real. There's even evidence from thousands of years ago of the Egyptians complaining about the different generations! It's just a cycle!

replies.author: @NicholasLeeson
replies.text: @SirBlackReeds Rome was brought down by a combination of things. One was their increasing complexity, which meant their return on their investments was low; another was each successive generation becoming spoilt and expectant; and another was the climate getting colder, where their culture peaked during the Roman Warm Period, but with then the climate cooling this resulted in less crops / less proper nutrition / less testosterone, etc, and where the cooling climate led to the Dark Ages period. Complex Civilisations have come and gone many times throughout our history.

replies.text: This is an extremely biased video. I agree many good movies come from the past, like Forrest Gump and Fight Club, but there are movies like Interstellar, The Wolf of Wall Street, Top Gun Maverick, Ford vs Ferrari, Arrival nowadays. They do not suck and they are not forgettable.

replies.author: @victorcates9330
replies.text: @1314special In the 60s, films were stagnating a bit. Directors started looking towards european art cinema and postmodern intellectual thought. Additionally, the vietnam war created a cynicism and opened the door to powerful relevant criticism of society. The result was the Hollywood Renaissance: probably hundreds of great films ranging from Taxi Driver to Blade Runner to Star Wars to Scarface to Jaws to Mash. And those director kept churning over movies. Hence Wolf of Wall Street. I think the issue isn't quality of movie. But if a movie revolutionizing the art or has something to say, then it's going to have impact. The rise of independent film in the 90s excited people. Fight Club, The Big Lebowski and Trainspotting felt zippy and creative. But the directors of those films are still buzzing around. They haven't been replaced. Villeneuvre and Robert Eggers are the current generation of auteurs, but nothing they've produced has been venerated as much as Apocalypse Now. The legendary actors of this era haven't hit the status of Al Pacino, De Niro, Jack Nicholson (who rose on those films). Di Caprio is up there, but he's about it.

author: @sterling7
text: The trend is even more apparent in video games, but I think the culprits are similar: You have a product so expensive to make that someone convinces you it has to appeal to _everyone_ to succeed, from Chinese government bureaucrats to Middle-American corn farmers to blue-haired social media zealots. You take no risks, you over-price the product, you double down by trying to sell a dozen other things at the same time (toys, fast-food meals, posters, cars...), and by trying to appeal to everyone, you end up appealing to no one. We have twenty- and thirty-theater megaplexes showing five or six movies.  We ought to be awash in small, independent, less expensive movies that are willing to only appeal to an audience that can actually pay their costs.

author: @christopherwatson4730
text: I've been saying to my kids that I feel sorry for them, they'll never get the opportunity to be amazed in cinema ever. They seem puzzled by that as they cannot understand the amazement we all shared at Jurassic Park, Terminator 2 etc.. I try to explain to them when I saw Saving Private Ryan at the cinema that it's the only film I've ever seen that when the credits rolled at the end the entire audience just sat there for a few moments in shock/awe at what they'd just witnessed. The point I'm making is that nothing is new for them, nothing can amaze them as they've got access to everything immediately. I cannot see the current trend changing and it astonishes me, my wife and I are trying to get them to watch older films, they enjoy them but are instantly bored at the credits at the start of the film and that in a nutshell is where the problem begins, patience. Young people now have none, they want or need instant gratification for everything. Sad times we live in now.

replies.author: @Isnortbologna
replies.text: I'm not saying you are, but make sure not to black pill them. I see many parents doing so. Explain the position they are in and then encourage them to be the change needed to turn it around.

replies.author: @MatthewCastgna-t9r
replies.text: I feel you. The experience is gone. Part of the overall impact, joy, memory making was getting ready to go to the theater or movie store, the smell of popcorn, the drive, walking around a video store and seeing both new things to peak your interest and old things that get you talking. Those experiences themselves compounded the joy of the movie experience, to say nothing of the stories and terrific writing we used to get in films and shows. Everything now is convenient and streamlined, as mentioned. There is no anticipation, no personal adventure. You just sit in a familiar room and look for something to stream that is more than likely not original or imaginative, not well written, and agenda driven. The experience that brought excitement and joy is gone. Sadly, convenience has replaced experience.

replies.author: @CJ-1776
replies.text: Everything is too 'safe' and follows 'the formula'.  It's just like the music industry.  Films/musicians don't take risks anymore, and when they do it's usually for shock value only.  I saw the new Superman this weekend and was completely uninspired by any of it.  Totally forgettable, CGI heavy, same ol' Marvel humor.  Blah blah blah..shiny things, explosions, a couple forced in jokes...blah blah blah.  It's just another 'Big Mac' in a giant pile of hamburgers.  The Jurassic franchise has been making almost the exact same movie over and over again since '93.  Science creates Dinos, greedy corporate types try to exploit dinos, dinos don't play along, chaos ensues.  Rinse, repeat.  There are good and even great films made in the past 20 years but man, you really have to go looking for them.  Most of 'modern entertainment' isn't really entertainment, it's just 'content'.

replies.author: @TheBenjomcmlxxv
replies.text: I played a few movies from my younger days with one of my girlfriends. Jurassic Park and Independence Day. (I like to put them up outside on the back of my garage, like a drive-in) When the aliens blew up those first buildings in Independence Day I was watching her. To see how she'd react.... Nothing. Stone face. When I saw that in the theater it melted my brain... Same with the dinosaurs in Jurassic Park. The issue is that these kids have seen it.  Not these exact examples, but things much bigger, and more elaborate. There was no way a Transformers Live Action movie could have been done when Jurassic Park came out. The technology simply didn't exist. Now every other movie has effects more spectacular than that. They're burned out on it and they don't even know it. It's just all visual noise to them. And they have no idea how well a practical effect can change the entire vibe of a movie away from green screen BS to real life extravaganza.

I watched blade runner recently. Sure its visuals were amazing, but that last monologue by Rutger Hauer’s character, “all those… moments… will be lost in time like… tears… in rain” will forever resonate with me. Haven’t seen a recent movie that can match that level of resonating.

replies.text: @likestoospooge quotables aren't the defining characteristic of a revolutionary movie. Heat came out in the 90s, I don't see many quotes from there that people still use today, even though Pacino gave us quite a few great ones. But you'll definitely remember those movies for the characters, or for the action. Interstellar and Inception at least are quite like that. I mean, when you think black hole, you think Interstellar, even though we have a real life picture as well. Not to mention how popular the score for that movie is even today. The question is, will these movies be remembered 20 years from now? For those ones, I think the answer is yes.

author: @jonathananderson3589
text:
My wife has a theory:  things were better when cocaine was in the Writer's Room.  SInce everything is decided by committee and data analytics, there's no room for the balls-out madness that created some of the greatest pieces of cinema ever produced.

I have no counter-argument to her idea, nor have I the slightest desire to find one.

replies.author: @Toshirotto
replies.text: That's not such a bad assumption, didn't the Pink Floyd frontman say he composed his best music when he was high?

replies.author: @jonathananderson3589
replies.text:
@Toshirotto I wouldn't be surprised.  I know that Stephen King has straight-up admitted that he cannot recall writing at least one entire novel - Cujo, I believe - because he was coked out of his mind.

Interesting that I haven't been as drawn into his works since he got clean.  Don't get me wrong, I'm happy for him for being sober, but I haven't found his work as compelling.

replies.author: @SeraphsWitness
replies.text:
@Toshirotto You might be thinking of Syd Barrett, the founding member of Pink Floyd, but went way off the rails because of his drug use and they replaced him with David Gilmour in 1968. Actually, all the other guys were clean (relatively, it still was the 60s/70s after all). In fact they wrote "Wish You Were Here" about Syd Barrett and his decay into drug culture. So most of their hits were created after Syd Barrett left.

But the sorta psychodelic themes were incidental to the time period. Pink Floyd wasn't actually known for their drug use.

author: @MrPrototype150
text: This is the result of data analysis. The data shows that people love something, and every company wants to add this thing because the data shows so, and when the movie is a big hit, they just add the same thing because.. well, the data shows so. Otherwise, when the movie flops, they don't try to create something new. They just look for other numbers that they think they didn't take into account, and so those numbers must be the key. This is why 99% of movies today feel like slop, it's because they are slop, filled with the same stuff everyone has already seen and without any new ideas because companies have no data on those new ideas exactly because they are new.

replies.author: @scottwyatt2614
replies.text: @wekurtz72 I've finally gotten used to the "green screen."  It used to be blue.  The specific color was "non-photo-reproductive blue."  It was used because cameras of the time literally could not record it.  IOW, it didn't have to be masked.  From set to screen it literally vanished.  Now it's green, they have to mask it out, and you can tell.

replies.author: @jagsdomain203

​@DoctorWooo 100 percent. Godzilla was a story about people not godzilla.  Other thing about Godzilla minus 1 was you could take Godzilla out and replace him with a burning building or storm or anything he did not matter.  The effects they did have were great.  They shaded out the Destroyers in fog so they could fudge in the effects.  As a history guy I am so glad they treated the equipment they were using in the correct way.  Also you could see Godzilla eyes.   
 Jaws shark did not work. Empire blizzard trapped everyone in a hotel so they cut a hole in the snow and told Hammel good luck act.
Blair witch no budget people enjoyed it.
When things don't work people innovate.
Today we don't have to innovate

replies.text:
it is funny how he pointed out about fashion and tech, recently I was thinking how it's funny in time travel shows and movies that people would stick out like a sore thumb because of their attire (even casual wear) and what subjects/objects would be out of place in a conversation. Back to the Future part 1 was set 30 years prior to Marty's home timeline, it's obvious what tech and styles exploded between the 50s and 80s. In Quantum Leap pilot episode, Sam is in the 50s (~30 years before the show) and he mentions an "electric razor" for shaving then his not-wife stares at him like something is growing out of his forehead
 However 30 years ago from today there aren't too many differences that surface level descriptions would make their head tilt in absolute confusion or disbelief such as: a smartphone because describing it and its functions would just create an image of a PDA simply having phone service added to it. The internet already existed. VR games already existed. Flat screen TVs already existed. Vehicle infotainments already existed. It would not be until you try to get into deep details of these things would it really stick out, especially to those who are working or interested in the tech field at that time and would understand the specifications and functions you're listing

replies.author: @tta-bln
replies.text: Agreed on the GoT, but more generally, I'm glad the notion that TV series have surpassed films is finally fading. I always disliked debating with friends who believed that "more is better"—that endless seasons somehow added value. In reality, most series just repeat themselves, offering quantity over quality and wasting time. Feature films remain superior: they deliver a complete, emotional journey in a concentrated form, exposing us to new perspectives, relatable experiences, and deeper insights about the world. Series, as a medium, are creatively exhausted. Film still reigns—just not in these recent decades of Hollywood’s moral decline. Fortunately, great cinema is being made elsewhere, and some of the best work appears at film festivals.

text: I'm so thankful to have grown up during a time of cultural plenty -- the 70s, the 80s and the 90s were an embarrassment of riches for people who read books, watched TV or went to the movies. replies.author: @Soldier4USA2005
replies.text:
Agreed. There is a reason why nostalgia works so well on those generations. It's because everything is boring and undefinable now, so we yearn for the days of real identity and "you had to be there" experiences from our childhood. Like playing outside with your friends until the street lights came on or your mother opened the door and yelled "Time for dinner!". Or riding our bicycles all over the city to go to places like the movie theater or Taco Bell or Pizza Hut ... when restaurants had identifying colors and themes. Or using pagers to send the phone number of the house you're at, so your friends or parents knew how to contact you. Or even using payphones.

What made our childhoods special has been washed away and replaced with instant gratification apps on $1500 phones, all leading to a society looking at a small screen their entire life. Such as using it to record a concert instead of just enjoying it.

author: @TheRainingWitch
text: There's a very good reason for this. Both of my parents are screenwriter's and novelists, very succesful in europe. They and many good storytellers like them don't want to work with the likes of Netflix etc anymore, because the contracts they have to sign means they no longer own the copyright, even if it's their original story. They've been in the business 50 + years, and they always owned copyright until the last 10 years. But Netflix et al wants to own the product and do whatever they want with it, sick a bunch of unexperienced writers on it to make 50 garbage episodes, or make it woke (which both my parents hate). So many experienced writers are chosing to just write books instead, which means that manuscripts are now pure garbage, for both film and series. And it's very hard to make a good film off a garbage script. That's why series like Game of Thrones did so well, they were based on books by a real author. Sucks for all of us who love movies, but unless the contracts change, talent isn't gonna come back to Hollywood.

replies.author: @LongJohnathan
replies.text:
​@gregshockDo yourself a huge favor and buy the audiobooks 👌 Comparing the books and the pitiful show is like comparing the Yankees to 6yo T-Ball. 

Had they done it right and not cut out at least 50% of all the main and side characters, Ned dying would've been  a non-issue for non-book fans like yourself. Jon Snow is 180° different, he's full of emotion and has an actual personality. There's a ton of other awesome characters up at the wall with him too. Tyrion is WAY more ruthless and violent and cunning, guy actually fought in battle with his mountain clansmen instead of being moronically knocked out and trampled on. His journey after leaving KL is buckwild. Varys is way more sly and secretive and absolutely isn't some goodie-two shoes-servant of the realm. Cersei isn't even remotely a "boss bisch" and experiencing every single decision she makes blowing up in her face is one of the most delicious literature experiences of all time. Jaime isn't some blind simp for "Mad Aerys With Teats' (Cersei). Stannis Baratheon is one of the baddest-coolest characters in the series, and unironically hilarious half the time with his wit and dry sense of humor. Brienne is far more endearing and humble and innocent. I could go on and on and on. And none of that even included Aegon Targaryen, Jon Connington, the Dornish plot, the Northern Conspiracy and Wyman Manderly and Roose Bolton, Mance Rayder going to Winterfell, Jaime down in the Riverlands with his host and the super cool characters alongside him like Strongboar Crakehall or Ser Flement Brax. Honestly, the only "boring" or "meh" parts are a lot of the Bran chapters, a few Brienne chapters later on, and a couple Sansa's. On top of it all, it actually feels like a Medieval world, with actual medieval dialect and terminology and battle tactics and weaponry being used, which the show cared little to nothing about.

author: @AaronMichaelLong
text:
I hate to be the bearer of bad news, but cinema is going to go the way of orchestras, ballet and opera. It's difficult for us to conceive of it, but consider this: There was a point when "Turandot" was a new Opera debuting in Milan; when "Swan Lake" premiered in Moscow; when "The Magic Flute" was performed for the first time in Vienna. When these art forms were at their peak of popularity and creativity. But now they're just relics of a bygone era. People will still sometimes buy tickets to watch another performance of the same work, but when was the last time a *NEW* ballet, a *NEW* opera or a *NEW* piece of orchestra (not the soundtrack for a film) caught the public imagination?

We are now at the tail end of the era of cinema. It's a dying art, shriveling up before our eyes, and the day will come when movie theatres become as marginal to our culture as the symphony hall or the opera house. Everyone is going to be playing video games and watching streamers and youtube videos, mostly of other people playing video games. Because that's the form of entertainment which is relevant to young people today. That's how they spend their time. The kids who grew up watching Ninja playing Fortnite aren't going to go to film school, they're not going to start making shorts with a camcorder. They're going to get a Twitch account, log into the latest hot video game, and start trying to gather followers.

I used to like going--if it was a good movie I wouldn't immediately forget and could talk about on the way home. Now movies are so bland you just wait for them to come out on streaming and watch them years later, because you're not missing out on any good conversation by putting it off. 

Even after watching a movie on streaming, there's not really anything to talk about because there's so little nuance in modern movies. It's all right there, nothing to discuss. One exception would be Dune, but that's just because it was a reasonably good adaptation of very interesting source material

author: @ingaz6565
text:
I went to a French bakery recently and got some freshly baked bread and croissant and my goodness was I in heaven!
Its a family owned shop with hand made from scratch, baked goods. Now at Walmart they got bread and croissants for half the price but its made almost entirely by machines and by the hundreds of thousands at a time.
I feel the same goes for modern movies, almost gone is the passion for movie making, the personal touch, that memorable scene or unforgettable sequence of events.  In its place are cookie cutter movies, designed for one thing and one thing only, bring in as much money as possible.
Art? Passion? Vision? Those directors are gone or getting to that age where they will soon be gone, in their place is a machine.

I’ve been a life long science fiction fan. I grew up reading the books from the Golden Age. When I was 25 years old in 1977 I watched “Star Wars” and 
I cannot begin to describe to you the thrill it gave me. Here, finally was a movie that matched and at times surpassed my imagination. That movie was made with practical effects. Now, as you say, it seems that they’re just trying to sell the movies on their CGI, more than on the story.

text: I think there is one thing overlooked here, there was less movies/shows 30 years ago so more people watched the same things, movies/shows werent always available, attention to movies wasnt as diversified more people watched the same thing so ultimately a movies ability to make a cultural impact was much higher 20-30 years ago. text: I think the biggest problem with modern entertainment is that it's too safe. Too manufactured. There are a set of rules that people fear to step out of. And I think the main reason for it is social media. People fear too much for other's opinions and forget the things that define their own character. Not that it's the only key. You have to put up the work to be competent enough to make it. And the sad thing is Artists now have shifted to two groups. Either too scared to get out of their shells or too confident and hasty to look back at their flaws and try to refine it.
